There's several ways that you can train your people throughout your organization. It depends on who they are, what they want to do. More importantly, if they are a B, an A, or a K, what kind of experience you need to give them in order to teach them. The very first thing I do to identify anybody in my organization is I make sure I understand. Are they a B, an A, or a K? Just in case you skipped over that course, visual, auditory, or kinesthetic. Is the person needing to be shown something? Do they need to hear something? Or do they need to feel the impact of something and take action with it right away? Now, as I identify in phase number seven, team building phase, how to train your people, it can be different depending on what you want them to learn and the level of expertise or advancement that they're already at. For example, I have a digital marketer. He trains himself over three hours each day on different material, ranging from what he's currently weak at to what he's interested in to different, I don't know, psychology things or marketing things or whatever it is that he's currently expressing interest in that he's personally training on himself. Because I know he trains that much, I know that I could spend money, for example, getting him an advanced course or sending him to an event that would benefit his skill set as a digital marketer. I identify that he has a weak copywriting skill set, so I'm able to take that and I'm able to go out and get him a bunch of books or get him a bunch of courses where I identify something like Grammarly that can increase and help him with copywriting skills. Funnel hacker, the funnel scripts or whatever it is, you can have something that gives you a little tool or a template that helps you build a foundational piece of knowledge for the weak area. But more importantly, there's different events, experts, friends and mentors that I'll hook him up with long-term and short-term to help benefit that skill. So I'll train him on a one-to-one level. Now, if I identify that several people in my organization need copywriting as a skill, I'll have a team training, which doesn't happen that often anymore, but nonetheless, it's because most of my training goes on an individual basis now. If you have a team training, it's because you want to have something that universally impacts the business, not just because you want to get it all done at one time. I found that team trainings typically have less concentration, they have less excitement, unless you have somebody who's really engaging whoever it is that is in the room. If you're like my team and you're spread out and it's a virtual meeting, it's hard to stay excited. We don't know if the people are actually sitting there watching or we don't know if they're doing whatever, smoking weed or something. We don't know, we have no clue. So that being said, when you're doing team training, I like to do it on a one-to-one level because I also can hold the person to count them. If I've done the material myself, I can ask the person any questions about it that are relevant to almost a test or just seeking if they need any questions answered or something from the material itself. Now, when you are training your people, I recommend sending them books commonly. Typically, people in my company receive maybe on every month or every other month based on some kind of book because I know they didn't really take longer to read than I would. I try to read a book a day, which I end up finishing in about a week or so. So, the moral of the story is, train your people, send them books, buy them courses, give them material, do it in a group training, send them to events as a reward to monetize not only their abilities to get results but to make them feel good about getting those results. I'm going to say monetize. I mean to expend something that way you save money at the end of the year that also monetizes your future impact on your ability to over deliver for clients. So, understand this, training your people makes them better at what they do. If they don't train themselves, train them. If they aren't taking in a certain type of training that you're providing them, try something else. And more importantly, identify their V and A or K. You can fit the category for how they need to learn it. That way you're not trying to shell a surplus into a square hole. You're identifying and matching with how they need to interpret information for their particular learning mechanisms. The best team trainings that I've ever done and that I've ever given have been on systems or databases that the team of universities uses as far as the impact of ROI in the business. When it comes to the individuals having higher levels of expertise, I can't tell you enough that Frank Kern's training material or any digital marketer that you're using is going to be some of the best material that you can have. Because it teaches them marketing automation while teaching them marketing and psychology at the same time while also depending on the type of bitches you're in help them generate leads. The moral of the story ladies and gentlemen, whatever it is that you choose to train your staff on, the solutions far and wide, everything and everybody in your organization on an individual level and on a team level. You as an entrepreneur and as the executive and as the digital agency owner are the one responsible for trying to find the best solutions possible and for scouting them for your team. This 90% falls under you being able to spend money on things like self-education and all these things that you spend money on now not only can train you and make you better, they can train your people and make them better and they're right off the long term. All that being said, make sure that you understand how to train your people is one of the most important things that you can do. Make them better, self-educate them, push them to do it and hold them accountable with the information that you provide. Create a community environment for your people to knock off new information from each other like, hey, what do you think of this? Hey, what do you think of this? Test them on it. Do an internal conversation where all you do is just simply talk about things and all the concepts that they've learned recently. Training your people is super important. Always become better because keep in mind, nothing stays the same. Things are either getting better or they're getting worse. What do your people do? I'll see you guys in the next phase.
